selected_themes.rationale: Five-way fault line on whether 'the West' refers to anything real is the sharpest conceptual split in the set and the audience's need for the concept to cohere makes it personally destabilizing; four strong activations anchor the human debate while Whanganui — whose temporal horizon dwarfs Western civilization entirely — forces the question outside the human frame, and Marley names the whole edifice Babylon from the margins it erased.

Four strong activations produce a genuine deadlock — spiritual crisis (Dostoevsky) vs. structural collapse of public space (Arendt) vs. designable process (Tang) vs. terminal institutional decay (Thiel) — with the highest audience friction in the set because their own organisations mirror the EU's democratic deficit; Octopus challenges the unified rational voter every other position assumes, opening the nonhuman axis on the most human of questions.

The structural-hypocrisy question hits the audience's complicity in extractive systems they critique; three strong activations split irreconcilably on whether hypocrisy is strategic survival (Cleopatra), existential poison for politics (Arendt), or a Western-centric crisis narrative the Global South has always lived (Ibn Battuta), while Scheherazade tests whether cunning fiction can protect the vulnerable, Whanganui speaks as an actual legal person within the order being diagnosed, and Marley testifies that the rules have always served the rulers.

The widest fault line in the set (six-way) combined with the most direct audience complicity — they design and fund the platforms diagnosed here — and four strong activations including the two most rarely heard voices (Scheherazade on whether story can do its slow moral work when attention is industrialised, Ada on formalising the problem before declaring it unsolvable), with Dostoevsky insisting no algorithmic fix reaches the soul and Octopus modelling distributed cognition as a genuine alternative to centralised information governance.

Grounds the preceding abstract legitimacy debates in specific suffering and the gap between stated principles and achievable outcomes; Cleopatra anchors realpolitik, Dostoevsky anchors one child's tear against territorial sovereignty, Scheherazade anchors who is silenced in the official peace narrative, Ibn Battuta brings cross-civilisational conflict-resolution precedent, Ada formalises the principle-practice gap to expose what rhetoric conceals, and Whanganui introduces the land itself as the stakeholder no peace table has ever included.

dropped_themes.reason: Score 2 with predictable fault line; policy-heavy content where multiple members go flat; sovereignty-under-pressure territory substantially covered by theme_003's sharper treatment of power asymmetry and structural hypocrisy.

coverage_check.axes_covered: Power-and-agency spans from intra-human institutional contestation (001, 002, 003) through the human–nonhuman boundary via Whanganui's legal personhood (001, 003, 006) and Octopus's distributed cognition (002, 005) to the question of whether centralised governance of information is even the right model (005); change-and-actor ranges from contested civilisational meaning where rupture and progression are themselves in dispute (001) through collective institutional crisis (002, 003) to individual suffering weighed against geopolitical abstraction (006), with 005 holding the sharpest tension between design-as-progression and existential rupture.
